title: Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street
author: Herman Melville
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street" book by Herman Melville?
Options:
A. Previously, I had only known a sadness that was not disagreeable. Now, the tie of our shared humanity drew me unavoidably to gloom. A brotherly melancholy! Bartleby and I were both children of Adam. I remembered the colorful silks and beaming faces I had seen earlier that day, fancily dressed and sailing down Broadway like swans. I juxtaposed them with the pale copyist, and thought, joy seeks out the light, so we assume the world is cheerful, but sorrow hides away, so we presume misery does not exist. These depressing reflections - surely delusions of an infirm and foolish mind - led to other more specific contemplations about Bartleby's peculiarities.
B. In the past, I had only experienced a sadness that was not entirely unpleasant. Now, the bond of our common humanity irresistibly attracted me to gloominess. A kindred despondency! Bartleby and I were both sons of Adam. I recalled the bright silks and shining faces I had seen earlier, decked out gaily and gracefully gliding down Broadway like swans. I compared them to the pallid copyist, and thought, happiness embraces the light, so we think the world is joyful, but misery conceals itself, so we believe misery does not exist. These melancholy notions - surely chimeras of an unhealthy and silly brain - resulted in other more particular thoughts about Bartleby's odd behaviors.
C. Previously, I had only felt a not-unpleasant sadness. Now, our shared humanity powerfully drew me to despondency. A brotherly melancholy! Bartleby and I were both children of Adam. I remembered the bright silks and beaming faces I had seen earlier that day, dressed up and gracefully sailing down Broadway like swans. I contrasted them with the pale copyist, and thought, happiness seeks out the light, so we believe the world is cheerful, but misery hides away, so we believe misery does not exist. These sad musings - surely delusions of an ill and foolish mind - led to other more specific thoughts about Bartleby's eccentricities.
D. Before, I had never experienced aught but a not-unpleasing sadness. The bond of a common humanity now drew me irresistibly to gloom. A fraternal melancholy! For both I and Bartleby were sons of Adam. I remembered the bright silks and sparkling faces I had seen that day, in gala trim, swan-like sailing down the Mississippi of Broadway; and I contrasted them with the pallid copyist, and thought to myself, Ah, happiness courts the light, so we deem the world is gay; but misery hides aloof, so we deem that misery there is none. These sad fancyingschimeras, doubtless, of a sick and silly brainled on to other and more special thoughts, concerning the eccentricities of Bartleby.
Answer:

Bartleby and I were both children of Adam. I remembered the colorful silks and beaming faces I had seen earlier that day, fancily dressed and sailing down Broadway like swans. I juxtaposed them with the pale copyist, and thought, joy seeks out the light, so we assume the world is cheerful, but sorrow hides away, so we presume misery does not exist. These depressing reflections - surely delusions of an infirm and foolish mind - led to other more specific contemplations about Bartleby's peculiarities.
